The failed attack at Gracie Mansion highlights the necessity of stronger public safety protocols in New York City.

MI's @Rafa_Mangual explains why federal prosecution is critical for keeping the city secure:

Video
For the latest from MI and @CityJournal, subscribe to our newsletter: manhattan.institute/subscrib…

NYC Faces Rising Extremist Threats

New York could face future extremist attacks, especially amid the war on Iran. MI’s @JMfreespeech reports in @CityJournal that the NYPD is monitoring streets, subways, bridges, and key infrastructure, using drones to support security efforts. bit.ly/3MZl0Zt

NYC School Safety Underreported

New York narrowed rules for reporting school violence, so serious incidents now appear far lower. Families can’t exercise their federal right to transfer kids from “persistently dangerous” schools, even though real safety risks persist, argues @neetu_arnold in @nypost. bit.ly/40N1RNH

Public Safety Under Strain

Saturday’s attack shows issues in Mayor Mamdani’s relationship with the NYPD. As @Rafa_Mangual writes in @CityJournal, New Yorkers can’t rely on luck—large gatherings need adequate police protection against terrorists and violent actors.  bit.ly/4sz4uOS

NYC Mayor and Saturday’s Attack

As NYC’s first Muslim mayor, Zohran Mamdani could speak out against Islamic extremism and promote unity. But by delaying acknowledgment of Saturday’s attack and ignoring the extremist ideology behind it, he failed that test, MI president @reihan writes in @TheFP. bit.ly/4bbET7m

Saturday’s attempted attack outside Gracie Mansion raises urgent questions about the future of public safety in New York City.

MI scholars weigh in on terrorism threats, policing, and what comes next.🧵

MI's @Jesse_Leg on @WillCainShow on today's synagogue attack:

" We're here again. A synagogue has been infiltrated by an armed gunman. This is the reality of life in America for Jews today."

Video

Manhattan Institute is now partnering with @realDailyWire to bring weekly commentary and research from our scholars on politics, culture, education, public safety, and the economy.

The series launches with MI president @reihan on the GOP’s path to 2028:

College athletics generate about $19B annually, but the system is under strain.

In a new MI report, @andrewperloff examines how recent legal and policy changes—especially name, image, and likeness (NIL) compensation and relaxed transfer rules—are reshaping college sports.

What does the Democratic Party’s coalition look like today?
